speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Aplonia Koedam, in Wyckoff, New Jersey, on November 21st, 1996; interviewer is Alexandra Isles. And the language is English. This is an interview with Aplonia Koedam, in Wyckoff, New Jersey. It's November 21st, 1996. And I'm Alexandra Isles, the interviewer. And our language will be English.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please state your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Aplonia Klara Bastiaanse-- my old name. And my marriage name is Koedam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell your maiden name, and spell your first name, and your maiden name, and your last name? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And say which each is. OK, the first name is A-P-L-O-N-I-A. And my middle name is K-L-A-R-A. And my last name is B-A-S-T-I-A-A-N-S-E-- is my maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your married name.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the last name is Koedam-- K-O-E-D-A-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My date of birth is 1915th on the December 26.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are born in Rotterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me a little bit about your family life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had an large family. We are really with 11 children. And unfortunately, one little brother died that was one year older than I am. And I was five years old at the time. So he died at six years old. That was very sad, of course. And I remember little things from that. Only really one thing I remember that-- that must have been the first or second day my mother must have been crying. And I imagine, or I thought, I saw something out of the window. I thought I saw a little cloud coming up. And I thought, oh, that was my brother going to heaven. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And though we were in a large family, we had a lot of fun together. We were not rich. My father was in the public service. And at night, at Friday night, he had an-- a part-time job. He did the lighting in a theater. And he did the lighting in the synagogue on Friday nights, that was. And also, liked he-- lighting in the theaters-- he really loved very much, because he was a music man. And this way, he watch all the operas. He knew them inside out about. So that's what I remember from my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His father was Cornel-- my father's name was Cornelis. And I remember little things such as what I said. We were not rich at all. And we had many children. And those little things I remember. He was always-- every night he was sitting in the kitchen or the dining area and fixing our shoes-- you know-- slowly sewing our children's shoes. Because we didn't have much to buy new shoes all the time. But we had an-- an-- a very-- a wonderful life. We all loved each other. Because the family was so large, it was more or less, you took care of each other. And my mother, herself, she always loved to be-- or a midwife, or a pharmacist. But you know, after she got married, what I said in my manuscript, she needed midwives. That's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you-- what number were you in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- let me see-- number seven. Another brother who died was a number six. So I was number seven. And we are four. I was really the middle part. In a way-- in a way, I-- not a lonely life, but it was like a group of three-- my three sisters, and my two brothers, and I came. To me-- one brother died then. And then there were the little group over there. They were the little ones. So even in a big family, sometimes you're not even in lonely life. Because I didn't really fitted in my age then, you know, and they are talking, or their-- their-- their hobbies in that part. And it didn't all fit in the other part either. Because my brother, who was just two years younger than I, he was very independent-- very independent. He would play on his own or walk on his own. And it was not that that we played together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were the three little ones. That was something, too. So it is strange, which is sometimes he said-- we were, of course, with all of us. But in my growing up, I was more by myself. And so how-- how did you spend time by yourself? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we played together, you know, we played together. And I always liked reading. And I played with my dolls. And as young as you are, you know, you look at the-- at the at the little ones. And yeah. But I'm-- and of course, even after school we played outside with girlfriends. That was really the playtime after school. And we came in. It was about suppertime. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would you say you were closest to in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Closest to in the family-- we were all very close. But I would say the close to us-- my brother Simon, and of course, very close to the little ones-- you had them-- two little. And I think I was-- from my sister I was close to the oldest sister, to [NON-ENGLISH]. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what role did the church play in-- in your life when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we were growing up, we-- you-- we did some youth work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Youth work like a girls club, and my husband in a boys club. And yeah, we children also went to-- in church for the youth, especially for the youth. We went to there. And then we went to Sunday school when I was growing up. I was a little bit a teacher in Sunday school-- a little bit, you know. I was not teaching, but I went to the children, you know. We had to learn a verse every week. And I found myself growing up, because they had to tell me what they remember of the thing. So that was, for me, to know, whole thing, or-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They had to memorize something? Is that what-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, every weekend they got something-- a nice thing when they knew it. So that was-- and then I said later we on worked in the girls club.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a lot of faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you talk about that a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father didn't talk too much about it. But he was a very faithful man. My mother had a lot of trust-- a lot of trust in God. And one thing I remember always-- when there was something during the war or-- she said, it will all-- it will all be all right. It will all be all right. We have our God. And one thing, I think later-- so much trust they had when my brother died. It was always believed, after death, a few years later, another boy was born. And they firmly believed that was God's way to present them with a new life they lost before it. So they always thought my brother, Joe, was replaced, because we lost Lennard. That was-- they firmly believed that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this-- when you went to school, was there any religion taught in school or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Well, yeah, once a week-- that was for the older children who were about maybe eight or nine years old. I remember once it was a college-- they call it in that-- in-- or a preacher, or an evangelist, whatever. Then they could attend that. It was one hour a week in the afternoon-- but it at this, no more in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you were growing up in Rotterdam--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: --was that where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of a Jewish community in-- in Rotterdam, or a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. There was also a-- a part where they prefer to live all together. And of course, we got more to know about Jewish people, because my father did the lighting in the-- he was-- I know at that time, even the song I always sang. I don't remember it anymore. But my father used sing it, you know. So we really were, when we were young, familiar with it. And for the rest of our life, people in business, or whatever-- they were Jewish. It was just no-- no problem. We never thought of it. We never thought of it. They were very happy. Most Jewish people were very happy living in the Netherlands. And what we knew from the Jewish people they were very loyal. They loved the royal family. They were very loyal to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were finishing school, what did you want to be? What did you-- what career did you-- were you planning on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always liked nice clothes-- [LAUGHS] --as young as I was. And I didn't get the nice clothes, of course. But I went, you know, and I took some lessons. And I went in apprenticeship in a-- in the fashion business. And we made dresses, suits, whatever. And our customers were mostly the ladies-in-waiting from the queen. So it was a kind of high professional thing at the time what I did with the company-- with the company-- with the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the queen at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wilhelmina.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was-- was this? Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoo-- shoo-- shoo-- shoo-- shoo-- shoo-- that was-- I think I started about 15. I was 15. So that was on about 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year did-- did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 18. So it was in 1928, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the boys club in-- in the youth work, we met. My brothers were in the youth work. And he was too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were often meetings once in while. Or we went camping, whatever. And so that's where we meet.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day we went for an bicycle ride. That was on the second day of Easter. In the Netherlands, you have two days-- Easter and the Monday is the second Easter day. Everybody goes out on bicycles and goes to the shore, go and do whatever. And it was decided. And then my husband was there too-- and other-- other people who worked in youth work. We went with a group. And my husband-- [INAUDIBLE] on the-- we paired off. And he said hello. And I said hello too, because I didn't even know him. And that is how we met. And he invited me. He had-- he build a canoe. He invited me to go canoeing in the lake near Rotterdam. And so it went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and could you tell us his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Cornelius Koedam.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- the youth work-- what-- could-- could you describe what that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youth work was also in the Christian manner. And they too, they took walks. And they had little outings. And then they had their meetings. And we tried to teach, you know, what is Christianity and the good works, of course, like in regular-- like boy scouts-- boy scouts, but then in Christian matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were-- what were some of the-- what would you say-- sort of key teachings and the-- the values?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the values is, you know, to love Christ, love God, and give yourself to that that you follow, you know, what-- for instance, the Ten Commandments-- for interest-- the little things. You know, you shouldn't do that. You shouldn't do that; and the love-- what we believed the love of Christ-- the love of God to humankind. And that was all done in an way that children could understand. And-- and always we had-- we had our outings. It was not only meetings. We had our fun too, you know, and nice evenings. They played games, or whatever. It was maybe more or less to keep what we here do-- people, young people off the streets, that they don't get in. That is really what the whole thing, what every, I think, children's organizations try to reach out. They were reaching out.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, I guess a little bit later, was the-- Nazism was beginning to rise in-- in Germany. Were you aware of that? What do you remember as your first awareness?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were so far aware of that. We have always known that Germany had really an-- a lot of militarism. We always knew that. But not, you know, one country has a other country. It doesn't. I was quite in-- I was quite in a friendly country. And we surely-- we are not-- what we found out-- equipped like they were-- when we found out. But there was really a tension for some years-- really a tension. And everybody knew that in Europe. As we, I say-- described in my manuscript-- the dark clouds hang over. And so often with the church, or something, the ministers, or the domination, they often mentioned, you know, that we have to pray. Because there is something, you know, that doesn't look good in Europe. But when that could-- it was for-- for-- for some years. It could never happen. Or it was, you know, that was just a tension-- that what is overall. And then when we got our marriage license-- we went into the municipal building in Rotterdam. We got our marriage license. And we came out. And all of a sudden, we saw Dutch soldiers walking around. Yes-- that day they mobilized.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They mobilized. It was on the end of August, 1939. And we had in three weeks a license. So that must be-- and we married on the-- September 13th. So that must be the last week of August in 1939. And then we realized the-- they had mobilized. All the military was called. And yeah, they were on the borders with nothing happened, nothing happened. But they just was-- the tension was too strong to-- we had to be prepared. But what was going to happen with the Nazis and so, we didn't know that much about it yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard anything about Kristallnacht or the persecution?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had you-- what had you heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- I think we heard that-- I don't know. I think we heard it even later. That I do not remember. That what-- what-- that was well known, the Crystal Night. That was in Poland, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- well, in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Germany--

speaker: interviewer
text: In '38 yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and Poland. yeah. It was in Germany, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get your information as to what was going on? Was it in newspapers, or radio, or what sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with newspapers and radio. But it was not-- not that we were aware that it's so-- was so close. We-- we knew the tension. But it was so close. And we really woke up [SIGHS] on the May 10th, 1940, because it was tension. We knew, but it never know. It could be years. But we had to, you know, watch our borders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe that day of May 10th, what you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, what I remember-- of course, we were-- newly married. And on that day, I-- my husband had a early shift. He worked as a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked as a-- was in the airplane building. And-- and that day, [SIGHS] that was around, oh, I think it was around 5:00. It was downstairs-- or 5:30. And I was making breakfast in the kitchen. And I heard-- it was still dark. And I heard an airplane flying over our homes-- and so  low, so low. And on it-- went off again. And my husband came downstairs. Let me see. Yeah, my [? husband ?] came. Then he came downstairs. And then we looked out the window. And we saw more and more coming. It was the-- the air was-- sky was still grey, and one after the other came over. And then all of a sudden, I found out the-- the gas stove [? shudder. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: The what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gas stove shuddering--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of course. And the doors rattled a little bit. And now we ran again to window. And I said, more and more are going. I said, we are at war. We are at war. It broke out. And [SIGHS] my husband-- and we knew. Right away my husband knew right away that was the place where he worked. Because it was an airfield too. Because we saw the planes go in that direction. And he quickly dressed. And he was in the first aid at the place where he worked-- his work place. So he went right away, dressed himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [SIGHS] then we realized that when he leaves, I was alone in the house. And we don't know when war breaks out, and they come in. You know, what can I do as a woman alone? We never had an experience of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we discussed a little bit. He said, yeah, maybe you better go home to the city where your parents live. We close up everything. Now you are not alone. And I will follow you after I am finished over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where were you when-- when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was where we lived. That was in-- in-- in-- in a suburb of Rotterdam. It was about with a bicycle, about a 1/2 hour riding.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and that's what we did. So he left. And we had our little toiletries with us. I packed a little toiletries and little things, and turned off everything in the house. And we left each our own way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Each on bicycles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on bicycles. So it was still a little darkish yet. And I bicycled. I was worried about [INAUDIBLE]. And it was so quiet on the road. It was too early. And then I came into the city. And I didn't see anything. I see nothing. Only I saw two men running. That's all. But for the rest, I think the town was still asleep-- the city-- nothing on the doors, people talking, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think they-- they hadn't heard. But I think maybe the wind was not their way. They did not hear-- had not heard the bombing, of course. I think that was the reason, because there was nobody out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And down there I was bicycling. And it came to a-- I have to cross two-- I had to cross two bridges to go into the city; and one bridge, and than just a little island, and another bridge. And I was relaxing more, little relaxing now. I was so far. Pretty soon I was in the city and go then home. And I was at that first bridge. And I was bicycling. And nobody was there-- no car.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, I see in the middle of the road a person. I think, what is he doing over here? He shouldn't be walking over there, because are pads for walking, walking, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I came closer. And it was a German soldier-- fully equipped. And he was-- and his belt full with his bullets. And he stopped me. Now my eyes popped. I got off. And I was nervous, of course. And he asked me for an Ausweis. That means identification. I said, I don't have on any Ausweis. Nobody wears, you know, wore an identification, whatever, or whatever. I said, I don't have that. Yeah, Ausweis, Ausweis! Yeah. I don't have it. And on there we stood-- awaiting, a pause. And then he looked at me. And I looked at him. He didn't know what to do. I think he was scared too, because he was the only lonely soldier on that big bridge. And then I looked at my watch. I had-- I had more or less-- I told him I had to go, you know, to the city. And he turned his head again. And then finally he said, OK. You go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was I relieved. I was nervous. I was shaking. [INAUDIBLE] was I relieved.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I [SIGHS] rode further on my bike, and on the second bridge came inside. And that was all fine. It was only a longer bridge-- a much longer bridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was almost on the end. And you know, 15 more minutes, and I'm home. And there is no problem here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to the ramp, and there was another one approaching me too. He stopped me. And the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think I got the idea already what to do. He let me go there. So I repeated the same thing. I did over there, look at my watch, and tell him, you know, I'm a housewife and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I looked at his eyes, I think he was very afraid too. He was the only one too. I think they were not older than 18 or 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a little bit, he let me go too. So then I went, you know, on my bike again, really relieved. And I didn't saw any soldier over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I later understood they were getting the British-- what we understood. So this-- the-- the city was fine again. I heard the trolley cars and some cars all over. Nobody, nobody was sad or what-- nobody. Then I came home. And I rang the doorbell. My family was still sleeping. They didn't know either.

speaker: interviewer
text: I rang the doorbell. They said, what is the matter? I said, it is war. What? I said, the war broke out. And that is how I came safely home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we turned on the radio. And then we heard everything. We heard on that radio. It was Radio Hildesheim, they called that. And-- and we heard our anthem. They sang the anthem. But they had a very strong accent.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, that were the Germans who got the station. And they addressed the people, and they sung our anthem. How dare.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we heard more. And we waited a little while. And then we heard some noise and more noise. And we tried again. And the anthem was sung again in Dutch. So they had a fight. And they took over again. They just took over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were just a few people who got our station, who had, of course, barged in or whatever, and took over. And they addressed our nation. But that was quickly fixed. They were gone again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And our own people had, you know, the news again. And then we heard the news. And it was the whole day true. You know, you can imagine the day. And people who worked-- and then we found out that they had taken the bridges. So people who worked on the other side of the city, where I came from, couldn't get through anymore. So I was just maybe one of the few who went over those bridges. And then more soldiers must have come in. And they were on the bridges. And that, of course, is a long story. You can, of course, tell a lot. But pretty soon, the marines, the Dutch Marines, were of course, got to know. And it was very close to the bridges. They had a caserne over there. And so the very first day, a big fight started. And it was a fight on the bridges. The Germans were on the bridges, and so were the marines. But the marines-- marines were very, very strong. We had five-day war. They fought five days on the bridges. They never got a hold of it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they never got hold of it, the Germans didn't get a hold of the bridges?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never got hold of it. No. And-- because they couldn't get a hold of it, that the bombing started for that. They had an ultimatum sent to one of the commissioners in Rotterdam. And they got a time limit that was set in broken Dutch. They got a time limit that they have to surrender, because it took too long. And while they were discussing it, that commander had no patience within the time limit he gave. He started bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna stop--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was around-- I can almost say that it was around a quarter after 1:00, I think. And that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop the first tape here.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was after five days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll pick--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was after five days.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just running out of tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I want to go back. And we'll--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
